title: Lesson 1: The Greatest Commandment - Loving God the Father
description: Everything in the Christian life flows from one command: love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength. But the command is not where it starts. It starts with a Father who loved you first. In this lesson you will explore the Greatest Commandment through an interactive diagram, encounter the pursuing love of God the Father, and see why your love for God is always a response to His love for you.

blocks.text: Welcome to Growing Deep

If you are opening this lesson, you have already laid the central foundations in the Starting course. Now it is time to grow deeper. Over the next three lessons, you will encounter the three persons of God and what each one means for your life. God the Father loves you. God the Son saves you. God the Spirit fills you. We begin here, with God the Father, and the most important question anyone could ask: of all God's commands, which one matters most? The interactive diagram below walks you through Jesus' answer step by step. Tap "Begin" and move through each stage slowly. Notice where the love starts and where it leads.

The diagram you just explored shows two truths that shape everything in the Christian life. First, God's love comes down. He moves first. He pursues. He initiates. Second, our love goes up. We respond with everything we are: heart, soul, mind, and strength. The vertical beam is the relationship between you and God. And when that love overflows, it moves outward to the people around you. That is the Greatest Commandment, and it is shaped like a cross for a reason.
The Shema (sheh-MAH): Love With Everything
When Jesus answered the question about the greatest commandment, He quoted one of the oldest and most sacred prayers in Israel, the Shema (pronounced "sheh-MAH"), from Deuteronomy 6:

Jesus answered, "The most important is, 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one. And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength.'"

The word Shema means "hear." It was the heartbeat of Israel's identity. Faithful families recited these words morning and evening. Parents taught them to their children. They wrote them on their doorposts. When Jesus pointed back to this ancient prayer, He was saying something that still shakes us today: the single most important thing you can do with your life is love God with everything you have. Not a ritual. Not a rule. Love. Total, holding-nothing-back, every-room-in-the-house love.

Heart, soul, mind, and strength are not four separate compartments. They are four ways of saying your whole self. Your heart is your deepest desires and loyalties. Your soul is the core of who you are. Your mind is your thoughts and reasoning. Your strength is your energy, your body, your daily effort. Jesus is saying: all of it. Nothing held back.

But here is the question most people never ask: How? How is that even possible? If we are honest, most of us can barely love God with part of our heart on a good day. So where does this kind of love come from?
We Love Because He Loved First
This is the part that changes everything. The Greatest Commandment is not the starting line. It is the echo.
We love because he first loved us.

Read that again slowly. We love because He first loved us. The command to love God is not asking you to generate something from nothing. It is inviting you to respond to something already given. God's love comes first, and your love is the echo. You cannot echo what you have not received. So the most important question is not "How do I love God more?" It is "Do I know how much the Father already loves me?"

Think of it this way: loving God is like getting a new tongue. When we taste something we do not like, we spit it out. We cannot help it. But if you got a tongue transplant, you might find yourself liking things you never could before. God does not just change our behavior. He gives us a new heart. What we once ran from, the love of the Father, His presence, His ways, we now want to run toward and be embraced by. New heart, new loves. What you want changes when who you are changes.
1.1 - Has anyone ever loved you in a way that changed how you treated them? How does that help you understand 1 John 4:19?
The Running Father

So what does the Father's love actually look like? Jesus told a story that answers that question better than any theology book could. Most people call it the story of the Prodigal Son. But it is really a story about the Father.

A young man asks his father for his inheritance early, which in that culture was the same as saying, "I wish you were dead." The father gives it to him. The son leaves, wastes everything, and ends up starving in a pig pen. He decides to go home and beg to be hired as a servant.
But here is the moment that matters most:
But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him.

The father was watching. The father ran. In that culture, a dignified man never ran, but this father did. He did not wait for an apology. He did not wait for the son to clean up. He ran, embraced him, and threw a party. The robe, the ring, the fattened calf: extravagant restoration, given before the son finished his rehearsed speech. That is the heart of God the Father toward you. He is not waiting for you to get your life together. He is running toward you right now.

There is a second son in the story, the older brother. He stayed home, kept all the rules, did everything right. And when the party started, he was furious. He had never left, but he had never received the father's love either. He had been managing his performance record instead of being a son. The father went out to him, too. The question the older brother could not answer is the same question this lesson is asking: Have you ever received the Father's love, or have you only been keeping score?
1.2 - Which son do you relate to more: the one who ran away, or the one who stayed home but never really received the Father's love? Why?
Boss or Father?

Too many of us see God the way an employee sees a boss. He hired us off the street. He is kind enough. But now that we are in the company, we had better stay in line. If we mess up, we are in trouble. But what about a Father who owns a small shop and brings you in, not because you are skilled, but because you are His? He knows you will struggle. He knows you will fail. But He says, "I want you here. And I will be right here with you the whole time." The security is not based on how well you perform. It is based on the love of the Father and your identity as His child.
The Boss is watching for mistakes. The Father is watching for you to come home. These are completely different relationships, and they produce completely different hearts.
What Changes Tomorrow?
There is a verse most believers do not know, and that is exactly why it lands so hard.
The Lord your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.

God does not merely tolerate you. He rejoices over you with gladness. He sings over you. This is the face of the Father: not disappointment but delight. Not distance but presence. Not a performance review but a love song. So here is the question worth sitting with all week: What changes tomorrow if God's face toward you is delight, not disappointment? What changes about how you handle failure? How you talk to yourself? How you pray?

When you encounter a Father like this, loving Him with everything stops being an impossible burden and starts being the most natural thing you can do. The Father's love does not lower the standard. It changes the heart that meets it. We are not fighting sin to have a relationship with God. We are fighting sin because we have a relationship with God.
For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments. And his commandments are not burdensome.
1.3 - Sit with this: What changes tomorrow if God's face toward you is delight, not disappointment? Write whatever comes to mind.
The Father, the Son, and the Spirit

One more thing before we close. Throughout Scripture, God reveals Himself as three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. This is what Christians call the Trinity. It is not three Gods. It is one God who exists eternally as three distinct persons, each fully God, each playing a unique role in your life.

In this lesson, you encountered God the Father: the one who made you, who pursues you, who runs toward you with delight. In the next lesson, you will encounter God the Son, Jesus Christ: the one who redeems you and gives you a new identity. And in the lesson after that, you will encounter God the Holy Spirit: the one who lives inside you and empowers your daily walk. The Father loves you. The Son saves you. The Spirit fills you. These three lessons belong together, and this foundation is what everything else in the course is built on.
This Week's Practice

Each morning this week, before you check your phone, read Zephaniah 3:17 slowly. Then sit quietly for sixty seconds with one question: If the Father's face toward me right now is delight, how does that change how I walk into this day? You do not need to write a paragraph or feel something profound. Just let the Father's love be the first thing that shapes your morning, before the noise rushes in.

Also this week: read Luke 15:11-32, the full story of the Prodigal Son. Read it slowly, and this time, watch the Father. Notice what he does, when he does it, and what it tells you about God's heart toward you.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
